Acceptance: add a unique-active-piece-ids invariant to src/main/gnostica/game_schema.cljc with a clear humanized error; add tests covering duplicate active ids, including cross-player duplicates; consider hardening fixture or seeding entry points if they can introduce duplicates.
close_reason: Added unique active piece id schema invariant with same-player and cross-player regression tests

title: Reconcile stale deferred Sword epic with completed Sword implementation
description: br list still shows deferred epic bd-15z claiming Sword gameplay logic remains to be implemented, including piece attack, territory attack, major edge cases, and browser staging. The current code, docs, tests, and closed child tickets indicate Sword command validation, piece/territory attacks, major variants, and browser staging are implemented. This stale open epic can mislead ready/deferred planning and future agents. Acceptance: close bd-15z if it is obsolete, or rewrite it to track only any genuine remaining Sword work with accurate child tickets.
close_reason: Closed obsolete deferred Sword epic bd-15z after verifying all Sword child implementation tickets are complete.

title: Split overgrown MIND_MAP nodes into smaller cross-linked entries
description: Several MIND_MAP nodes, especially 9, 10, 12, and 21, are very dense single-line paragraphs covering multiple subsystems. The file describes itself as a compact line-oriented knowledge index, but these nodes are hard to scan and risky to update atomically. Split renderer, move selection, card zones, test coverage, and icon-stack details into smaller cross-linked nodes while preserving the 20-50 node target.
close_reason: done

title: Align local and upstream rules-source references
description: README.md links only to the upstream Gnostica PDF, while MIND_MAP.md node [7] names docs/rules.txt as the current rules reference. Make the docs agree: point README readers at the local rules file used for implementation context, preserve the upstream PDF as provenance, and state which source should be treated as authoritative when they differ.
close_reason: Aligned README, AGENTS, and MIND_MAP rules-source guidance around docs/rules.txt with the upstream PDF retained as provenance.

title: Implement challenge win-or-elimination endgame rule
description:
docs/rules.txt says that at the end of a player turn, they may announce that their next turn will be their last. After that next turn, if the challenger has 9 points or more they win; otherwise they are eliminated, all their pieces are removed from the board, and their hand is discarded. A player may not announce a last turn while another unresolved last-turn challenge is pending.

Create or harden the browser-free gameplay logic for this challenge flow. Current project docs mention end-turn/challenge support, so this ticket should audit the existing implementation and fill any gaps rather than duplicate working behavior.

Acceptance criteria:
- A current active player can explicitly claim/announce a final-turn challenge only at a legal end-of-turn point.
- The default target score remains 9 points, while existing configured target scores 8 or 10 continue to work where setup allows them.
- While a challenge is unresolved, other players cannot announce their own final-turn challenge.
- After the challenger completes their next turn, scores are refreshed from controlled territories; meeting or exceeding the target records a winner and finishes the game.
- If the challenger misses the target, they are eliminated, their hand is discarded, all their active pieces are removed with stash mirrors reconciled, and turn order skips them afterward.
- If failed elimination leaves only one active player, that remaining player is recorded as winner.
- Structured success/error results, history events, schema invariants, and card/piece/stash accounting are preserved.
- Add focused unit and/or feature coverage for challenge announcement, challenge victory at 9 points, failed-challenge elimination, blocked overlapping challenge announcements, last-active-player win, and rejection after the game is finished.
close_reason: Hardened challenge claiming through the end-turn path and added coverage for public challenge claims, configured target scores, failure elimination, last-active wins, and finished-game rejection.

title: Split gameplay transitions from game-state core
description:
Fresh-eye cohesion review: src/main/gnostica/game_state.cljc is 1889 lines and owns setup/deck validation, player/stash mutation, Cup resolution, Rod resolution, draw, orient, initial placement, and turn advancement. Rod resolution/application spans roughly lines 487-853 and 1316-1647; Cup spans roughly 854-1315 and 1650-1685. The namespace is now both a state utility module and a rule catalog, so shared helpers such as piece coordinate and stash mutation are harder to reason about.

Acceptance criteria:
- Keep the public gnostica.game-state API stable as a facade.
- Extract setup/state utility helpers from move-family transitions.
- Move Cup, Rod, draw/orient/initial-placement logic into focused browser-free namespaces or equivalent focused modules.
- Update tests/features to exercise the public facade and focused helpers where useful.
close_reason: Split game-state into a stable facade, shared core helpers, and focused browser-free transition namespaces; added focused namespace compatibility coverage.

title: Mask bid selections until reveal
description: The setup rule says players select a bid card and put it face down before reveal. The current local lobby select remains visible with the chosen card title, so later players at the same screen can see earlier bids before Reveal bids. Acceptance: once a player chooses a bid card, the shared lobby surface masks that choice until the reveal step, still allows the choosing player to change or clear their choice intentionally, does not expose selected card titles in the lobby view used by the shared UI before reveal, and adds focused view-model or CLJS rendering coverage.
close_reason: Implemented masked pre-reveal lobby bid selections with view-model coverage

title: Add testable game and player state foundation
description:
## Goal
Introduce the pure shared state foundation for actual Gnostica gameplay, beyond the current board renderer.

## Requirements
- Model a complete game value in .cljc data that can be constructed, inspected, and transformed without UI or random side effects.
- Represent two to six players, turn/order metadata, draw/discard areas, the existing 3x3 board, Icehouse piece ownership/stashes/board positions, and per-player hands.
- Keep all rule transitions as pure functions with injectable randomness/deck order so examples and Cucumber scenarios can be deterministic.
- Design for extensive tests, including unit tests, Gherkin/Cucumber feature scenarios, and optional Malli schema validation/generation.

## Success Criteria
- Child tickets define the initial state model, player setup, schema validation, and feature-test harness.
- The completed foundation gives later rule tickets a stable state contract to test against.
close_reason: All state foundation child tickets are complete

title: Define pure game state model and transition contract
description:
Goal: create a shared .cljc namespace for the authoritative game state value and the pure helpers that construct and transform it.

Requirements:
- Reuse existing gnostica.board and gnostica.pieces data instead of duplicating renderer-specific state.
- Represent phase, players, current player/order metadata, board, pieces/stashes, draw pile, discard pile, setup/bid hooks, and any small event/history data useful for debugging tests.
- Construct initial state from explicit player specs plus injectable deck order or shuffle function.
- Expose rule transitions as pure functions with deterministic inputs and clear success/error returns.
- Enforce two to six players at the state-construction boundary.

Acceptance criteria:
- Unit tests cover deterministic construction, phase/current-player shape, deck injection, and invalid player counts.
- Public helpers are usable from clojure.test, Cucumber step definitions, and re-frame events without browser dependencies.
- MIND_MAP.md and README.md are updated if new state namespaces or commands are added.
close_reason: Implemented pure shared game state contract with deterministic construction and tests.

title: Implement player setup with six-card hands
description:
Goal: represent each player as testable data and deal six tarot cards to every player during setup.

Requirements:
- Player maps include a stable id, display name/color, hand, stash or available pieces, and small future-facing fields for scoring/challenge/elimination only where they clarify later rules.
- Setup accepts exactly two, three, four, five, or six players.
- Starting deal gives each player exactly six tarot cards before the nine-card territory board is dealt.
- Deck accounting is explicit: all dealt hands, the nine board cards, remaining draw pile, and discard pile account for the 78-card tarot deck with no duplicates.
- Tests can inject a known deck order and assert exact hands/board cards.

Acceptance criteria:
- Tests cover setup for two through six players and rejection of fewer than two or more than six.
- Tests verify every starting hand has six cards and no card appears twice anywhere in game state.
- Existing board and piece tests still pass.
close_reason: done

title: Add Malli schemas for gameplay state validation
description:
Goal: allow Malli schemas to validate key gameplay state values and support future fixture/generated tests.

Requirements:
- Add Malli as a dependency, or create a clearly scoped schema namespace once the implementation ticket selects the dependency version.
- Define schemas for card references, board cells, Icehouse pieces, player state, six-card hand limits, draw/discard piles, and the top-level game state.
- Provide small helpers such as valid-game?, explain-game, and assertion wrappers suitable for clojure.test and Cucumber step definitions.
- Keep schemas tied to pure data namespaces, not Three.js or re-frame view state.
- Use schemas at boundaries and tests; rule functions should still express gameplay logic explicitly.

Acceptance criteria:
- Tests demonstrate that generated setup state validates for two to six players.
- Tests cover representative invalid states such as too few players, too many players, a hand over six cards, duplicate cards, and a piece owned by an unknown player.
- Schema explanations are readable enough to include in failing Cucumber step output.
close_reason: Implemented Malli gameplay state schemas and validation tests.

title: Add Cucumber feature harness for gameplay rules
description:
Goal: make complex gameplay rules testable through Cucumber/Gherkin .feature files in addition to focused clojure.test unit tests.

Requirements:
- Add or document a Clojure CLI test command that runs feature scenarios alongside the existing clojure.test suite.
- Choose a runner or bridge that executes .feature files and calls Clojure step definitions without requiring the normal npm-free browser workflow to change.
- Store feature files under features/ and step definitions or support code under test/ or another documented test namespace.
- Provide reusable test-world helpers for deterministic deck order, player setup, action application, state lookup, and Malli validation checks.
- Add an initial setup feature covering two to six players, six-card hands, nine board cards, and complete deck accounting.

Acceptance criteria:
- The documented command runs both existing unit tests and the first feature scenarios.
- Failing feature steps report the scenario, step text, and useful game/schema explanation.
- README.md, AGENTS.md, and MIND_MAP.md document where future gameplay feature tests belong.
close_reason: Implemented gameplay feature harness and setup feature

title: Render only present Gnostica card icons
description: Cards with one or two Gnostica icons should render only those icons. The UI and Three.js card texture path should not pad icon triplets with blank placeholders that make every major-card overlay occupy three icon slots. Acceptance: shared icon/card metadata can represent one-, two-, and three-icon cards without blank sentinel icons; DOM board/hand/discard card surfaces render exactly the present icon count; Three.js generated major-card textures draw exactly the present icon count; tests cover one-icon, two-icon, and three-icon cards plus the absence of blank icon rendering.
close_reason: Implemented variable-length Gnostica icon stacks and verified exact DOM/Three.js icon counts.

title: Remove positional fragility from move-panel re-frame view wiring
description: The browser :gnostica.app/move-panel-view subscription in src/main/gnostica/app/events.cljs enumerates dozens of input subscriptions and then relies on positional vector destructuring before rebuilding the map expected by app-state/move-panel-view-model. This is easy to miswire when adding one option or control; the project already had a closed regression where control groups were dropped from this path. Evidence: events.cljs around 980-1102 mirrors app_state.cljc around 1387-1467 by position rather than by named data. Acceptance: route the browser subscription through app-state/move-panel-view where practical, or build named dependency maps instead of long positional vectors; add parity coverage that compares the re-frame composed view shape with the app-state facade for representative move stages.

title: Move README game-schema notes out of the keyboard-shortcuts section
description: README.md:57 describes gnostica.game-schema immediately after the Keyboard Shortcuts section without its own heading, so schema guidance appears to be part of the hotkey docs. Add a Gameplay Schema or Validation heading, or fold the paragraph back into Shared Game State, so the README structure matches the content.
close_reason: done

title: Highlight drag target while dragging a piece to the board
description:
When the user drags a piece toward the board, the territory card or wasteland space currently under the pointer should be highlighted dynamically before drop so the landing target is unambiguous.

Context:
- Direct manipulation already stages piece and stash-piece drag intents and exposes legal/disabled target descriptors.
- The board should visually track the current hover target during dragover/pointer movement, not only after a pending move is staged.
- This should apply to both territory cards and wasteland spaces where piece drops are meaningful.

Acceptance criteria:
- During an active piece or first-piece drag, the territory or wasteland under the pointer receives a visible hover/preview highlight.
- The highlight updates as the pointer moves between territories, wastelands, disabled targets, and empty non-target space.
- Legal and disabled target states remain visually distinct and use the existing move-legal-target descriptor status where available.
- The behavior works in both the Three.js board and CSS fallback board.
- The highlight clears on drop, cancel, dragleave, or drag end without mutating :game before confirmation.
- Existing click, keyboard, Detailed entry, and pointer-drag-disabled workflows remain usable.
close_reason: Implemented dynamic board target hover highlighting for piece and first-piece drags in Three.js and CSS fallback.

title: Question mark hotkey to show a help dialog of hotkey commands
close_reason: Added ? keyboard command help dialog with Esc close behavior and smoke coverage.

title: Normalize feature-test wording around the custom Gherkin runner
description: README.md:33 and README.md:57 plus AGENTS.md:21 and AGENTS.md:33 still say future Cucumber steps, while features already run through test/gnostica/feature_runner.clj with no Cucumber dependency. Replace future Cucumber wording with the actual custom Gherkin-style feature harness and keep README, AGENTS, MIND_MAP, and features/game_setup.feature consistent.
close_reason: Normalized custom Gherkin feature-runner wording

title: Expose bid-card redraw choices in lobby bidding
description: docs/rules.txt says the player to the right of the winner draws from any of the bid cards until his hand has six cards again, then each player does the same counterclockwise. The pure starting-bids contract supports explicit :redraws, but the browser lobby currently auto-generates redraws from the bid-card vector and never lets players choose from the remaining bid pool. Acceptance: after a winning bid, the lobby stages redraw choices in the existing game-state redraw order, shows only remaining bid cards for the active redraw player, confirms through apply-starting-bids with the selected redraws, preserves nil top-level :game until final confirmation, and adds app-state tests for choice order and invalid/duplicate redraw prevention.
close_reason: Implemented lobby bid redraw choice staging

title: Break up Three.js board lifecycle and controls
description:
Fresh-eye cohesion review: src/main/gnostica/three_board.cljs also has a 150-line mount! around lines 880-1029 and an 84-line scene component around lines 1030-1113. Together they handle renderer creation, scene graph assembly, pointer raycasting, hover/focus state, resize, keyboard panning, OrbitControls, disposal, and smoke metadata. Rendering, input, camera state, and cleanup are interleaved enough that behavior changes are hard to isolate.

Acceptance criteria:
- Extract scene assembly, pointer selection, camera/keyboard controls, and resource disposal into focused helpers or namespaces with explicit data contracts.
- Keep the Reagent component as orchestration with minimal imperative code.
- Preserve current smoke metadata and mount/update/unmount behavior.
close_reason: Split Three.js board lifecycle, controls, pointer selection, scene assembly, runtime checks, and cleanup into focused namespaces while preserving release and smoke behavior.

title: Death two-action shortcut detection misclassifies row/col territory targets
description: Backend Sword/Death flow decides whether two Death sword actions are a same-target shortcut before normalizing territory targets. same-action-target? compares only :board-index for territory targets, but valid commands can target territories by :row/:col. Two different row/col territory targets both have nil board-index, so they are treated as a shortcut and later fail with :invalid-death-shortcut instead of applying sequentially. Equivalent board-index commands succeed. This is in src/main/gnostica/game_state/sword.cljc around same-action-target? and apply-death-move. Acceptance: normalize or resolve territory targets before Death shortcut detection, or compare row/col targets without nil equality; Death can apply two Sword actions to different territory targets specified by row/col; same-target row/col territory actions still take the intended shortcut path; add regression tests for both cases.
close_reason: Fixed Death row/col territory shortcut detection and added regressions.

title: Hand-trade majors cannot take the optional trade-only path
description: docs/rules.txt states all powers are optional and uses Moon as the example that later powers may be used without earlier powers. Justice and Hanged Man both include a hand-trade power, but trade-only is not consistently playable. Pure Justice routes any command with :hand-trade-target through apply-justice-sword-move and then always calls apply-single-sword-move, so a valid trade-only Justice command fails with :invalid-sword-target. Pure Hanged Man can do trade-only when called with an explicit [:trade-hand] action, but browser move selection always stages Rod before trade for :hanged-man and never exposes the trade-only sequence. Support optional hand-trade-only for both pure and browser flows while preserving ordered trade-then-Sword or Rod-then-trade when both powers are chosen.
close_reason: Supported trade-only Justice and Hanged Man paths in pure gameplay and browser move selection, with regression tests and docs.

title: Allow lobby bid redraw selections to be corrected
description: The official bidding redraw UI in src/main/gnostica/ui/lobby.cljs:141 lets each redraw player pick a replacement card, but selected redraw cards have no remove or replace path before final confirmation. src/main/gnostica/app_state.cljc:642 also ignores blank card ids, so a misclick requires canceling and restarting bidding. Acceptance: selected redraw cards can be cleared or replaced before Start game, the active redraw player and available card pool recalculate correctly, and browser-free app-state tests cover select then clear/change.
close_reason: Implemented lobby bid redraw clearing/reselection with app-state coverage.

title: allow the game view panel to become larger, other panels should minimise when not in use
close_reason: Implemented wider game view layout with compact idle panels.

title: Add movable 3D board view
description:
## Goal
Add an optional 3D view of the current 3x3 Gnostica board where the camera can move around the card layout.

## Constraints
- Use Three.js from a CDN script URL, not npm.
- Keep the existing shadow-cljs workflow npm-free.
- Reuse the existing board data model from src/main/gnostica/board.cljc.
- Preserve the current rules invariant: nine face-up cards, 3x3 layout, small gaps, and portrait/landscape alternation between orthogonal neighbors.

## Success Criteria
- The app can render the current board in 3D.
- The camera supports orbit/pan/zoom or equivalent movement.
- Card textures use the existing tarot image assets.
- Selecting a 3D card updates the existing selected-territory panel.
- The 2D board path remains available or can be restored without large rewrites.
close_reason: Implemented and verified. clojure -M:test passed (6 tests, 27 assertions); clojure -M:release completed with 0 shadow-cljs warnings. Headless Chrome/CDP smoke checked desktop 1280x900 and mobile 390x844 with CDN Three.js r128, OrbitControls present, one nonblank screenshot-sampled canvas, no texture errors after card manifest fix, and center-card picking updated the selected territory panel.

title: Load Three.js from a pinned CDN script
description:
Goal: add Three.js to the browser app without introducing npm or package.json.

Requirements:
- Use a pinned CDN URL in src/main/resources/index.html or an equivalent static script entry point.
- Do not add npm dependencies or make npm part of the default workflow.
- Document the chosen CDN URL and version in AGENTS.md or README.md if it affects development.
- Add any externs or JS interop notes needed for advanced compilation.

Implementation notes:
- Prefer a stable, version-pinned CDN URL instead of an unversioned latest URL.
- If OrbitControls are used later, plan for a CDN-compatible approach as well.

Acceptance criteria:
- The app can access Three.js in ClojureScript at runtime.
- clojure -M:release succeeds with 0 shadow-cljs compiler warnings.
- The existing 2D board still renders after the script is added.
close_reason: done

title: Render the 3x3 board as a Three.js scene
description:
Goal: render the existing initial board model as textured Three.js card planes.

Requirements:
- Reuse src/main/gnostica/board.cljc as the source of truth for row, column, card, face, and orientation.
- Render exactly nine face-up cards in a 3x3 layout.
- Preserve the board invariant: every orthogonally adjacent card has the opposite orientation.
- Preserve a small visible gap between every adjacent pair of cards; cards must never touch.
- Use existing tarot image assets as card textures.
- Keep card dimensions consistent between portrait and landscape cards; landscape cards should be rotated, not distorted.

Acceptance criteria:
- A Three.js canvas shows all nine cards face up.
- Portrait and landscape cards alternate in the same pattern as the current 2D board.
- Texture loading failure is visible or logged clearly enough to diagnose missing assets.
- The scene can be mounted/unmounted cleanly by the Reagent component lifecycle.
close_reason: Implemented Three.js textured 3x3 board scene

title: Add movable camera controls to the 3D board
description:
Goal: let the player move the camera around the 3D board.

Requirements:
- Support orbit and zoom at minimum; pan is desirable if it feels stable on desktop and touch devices.
- Keep the whole 3x3 board visible by default on initial load.
- Clamp camera distance and pitch so users cannot easily lose the board or clip through cards.
- Do not add npm dependencies for controls. Use a CDN-compatible OrbitControls source or implement a small local control layer.
- Preserve responsive behavior when the viewport changes size.

Acceptance criteria:
- Mouse or trackpad users can rotate and zoom the board view.
- Touch users can move the camera on mobile-sized viewports.
- Resetting or reloading the app returns to a useful default camera angle.
- clojure -M:release succeeds with 0 shadow-cljs compiler warnings.
close_reason: Implemented OrbitControls-based camera orbit and zoom with clamps, reset, responsive resize, and advanced compilation externs.

title: Connect 3D card picking to selected territory state
description:
Goal: make cards in the 3D board selectable and keep the existing selected-territory panel in sync.

Requirements:
- Use Three.js raycasting or equivalent hit testing to identify clicked/tapped card planes.
- Dispatch the existing selected-board-index event or a compatible re-frame event when a 3D card is selected.
- Show a clear visual selection state in the 3D scene.
- Do not break keyboard or pointer behavior in the current 2D implementation if a 2D fallback remains.
- Keep board cell IDs/indexes tied to src/main/gnostica/board.cljc data rather than duplicating position logic in the renderer.

Acceptance criteria:
- Clicking/tapping each 3D card updates the title, position, and orientation in the territory panel.
- Selected card highlighting is visible from the default camera angle.
- Selection still works after camera movement and viewport resize.
close_reason: Implemented Three.js raycast card picking, re-frame selection dispatch, and visible selected-card highlights.

title: Verify and document the CDN-based 3D board view
description:
Goal: add verification and documentation for the first 3D board implementation.

Requirements:
- Document how Three.js is loaded from CDN and why npm is intentionally not used.
- Update MIND_MAP.md with any new renderer namespace, lifecycle, and interop notes.
- Keep tests for the pure board invariant in Clojure; add any pure board-to-scene mapping tests if mapping helpers are introduced.
- Run clojure -M:test and clojure -M:release.
- If browser automation is available, capture or inspect desktop and mobile viewports to confirm the canvas is nonblank, cards are visible, and controls work.

Acceptance criteria:
- Documentation names the CDN-based Three.js loading approach.
- Automated checks pass.
- Manual or automated smoke notes cover: nine visible cards, alternating orientation, small gaps, movable camera, and 3D card selection.
notes: Verification: clojure -M:test passed with 5 tests and 24 assertions; clojure -M:release completed successfully. Headless Chrome smoke-tested the released app at 1280x900 and 390x800: Three r128 and OrbitControls loaded from CDN, no fallback rendered, canvas was nonblank, nine textured cards were visible with alternating orientation and small gaps, drag/zoom changed the rendered canvas, reset was visible, and 3D card clicks updated the territory panel.
close_reason: Documented CDN Three.js loading and recorded passing test, release, and desktop/mobile 3D board smoke verification.

title: Preserve Three.js camera view across board and piece remounts
description: src/main/gnostica/three_board.cljs:120 remounts the Three.js board after cell, piece, or direct-manipulation prop changes without passing controls/capture-view-state. Only card icon mode changes preserve the view. A normal move can therefore reset a panned or zoomed camera, which makes repeated board interaction jumpy. Acceptance: capture and restore camera/orbit state for ordinary board and piece remounts unless an explicit layout reset is requested, and add smoke or component coverage that pan/zoom survives a piece update.
close_reason: Preserved Three.js OrbitControls camera state across board cell, piece, and direct-manipulation remounts; added smoke coverage that pans/zooms before a confirmed Three.js piece update and fixed CSS fallback drag-hover status to use current legal descriptors. Verified with clojure -M:test, clojure -M:release, and clojure -M:smoke.

title: Close the Cup wasteland gap in browser move selection
description: README.md:43 and AGENTS.md:23 document apply-cup-move with wasteland targets and one-point-card ids. The pure transition is tested, but the browser move panel in app_state and app only stages target-board-index and move-target-board-options, so users cannot choose a wasteland or one-point card for Cup territory creation. Either add that browser selection path or explicitly document the current pure-only limitation where move selection is described.
close_reason: done

title: Split move-selection state machine out of app-state
description:
Fresh-eye cohesion review: src/main/gnostica/app_state.cljc has grown to 1275 lines and mixes app-db init/selectors/help flags with the staged move-selection engine. The move engine spans source metadata and prompts around lines 24-107, requirement validation and staging around 548-738, selection mutation around 769-1076, option derivation around 1086-1127, and command construction/dispatch around 1133-1266. This makes every new move power a cross-cutting edit in one namespace.

Acceptance criteria:
- Introduce a browser-free move-selection namespace that owns requirements, staged params, option derivation, command construction, and confirmation result handling.
- Keep gnostica.app-state as the app-db boundary and delegate move-selection calls through a small facade.
- Preserve current app-state tests or split tests to match the new boundary without losing rejected-selection coverage.
close_reason: Extracted staged move-selection state machine into gnostica.move-selection with app-state facade and preserved app-state coverage.

title: Render Death Sword action-count controls in the move panel
description: Death and World-copied Death need a visible 1/2 Sword action selector in the browser move panel. Evidence: move_selection emits a {:type :sword-action-count} control group for Death, and app-state exposes :sword-action-count-options, but src/main/gnostica/ui/move_panel.cljs render-control-group has no :sword-action-count branch. clojure-lsp also reports sword-action-count-choices as unused and sword-action-count-options as an unused binding. Browser users cannot choose the Death action count even though tests call app-state/set-move-sword-action-count directly. Acceptance: render the existing sword-action-count-choices for this control group and add coverage that would fail if any emitted control-group type renders nil.
close_reason: Rendered Death Sword action-count controls and added renderer coverage for emitted control groups

title: Epic: Browser support for implemented major powers
description: Pure gameplay facades exist for draw majors, ordered/composite suit majors, Sword-related majors, and World delegation, but browser move selection only confirms a subset of these powers. Track the remaining staged UI flows so implemented rules stay visible in product planning while intentionally unwired powers keep returning explicit unavailable results.
close_reason: All child browser major-power support tickets are closed; README, AGENTS, and MIND_MAP reflect the implemented browser staging and unavailable-state behavior.

title: Wire browser staging for draw/discard major powers
description: Expose staged browser controls for Fool, High Priestess, and Judgement sources. Build commands for apply-fool-move, apply-high-priestess-move, and apply-judgement-move with source cost once, reveal/redraw/discard-pile choices, deterministic shuffle injection through the app event boundary, structured gameplay errors, and focused app-state tests.
close_reason: Implemented browser staging for Fool, High Priestess, and Judgement draw-major powers with app-state tests and docs.

title: Wire browser staging for ordered composite suit majors
description: Expose staged browser controls for Empress, Emperor, Lovers, Chariot, Hanged Man, and Temperance as ordered major-sequence powers rather than only their individual suit subsets. Build apply-empress-move, apply-emperor-move, apply-lovers-move, apply-chariot-move, apply-hanged-man-move, and apply-temperance-move commands with ordered actions, promoted minions, source cost once, structured errors, and app-state tests for representative hand-card and territory sources.
close_reason: Implemented ordered composite suit-major browser staging

title: Wire browser staging for Sword-related major composites
description: Expose staged browser controls for Justice, Death, Tower, and Moon as implemented major powers rather than only the plain Sword subset. Build commands for hand trade plus Sword, one/two Sword actions with shortcuts, Tower minion orientation and discard-pile replacements, and Moon Rod-then-Sword sequencing, preserving explicit unavailable results for any subflow not included in the first UI pass.
close_reason: Implemented browser staging for Justice, Death, Tower, and Moon Sword-related major powers with app-state tests and docs.

title: Wire browser staging for World delegation
description: Expose a browser move-selection flow for The World. Let the player choose a legal non-World major territory from world-major-territories, then stage the copied card's implemented command fields while using World as the paid source. Confirm through apply-world-move, reject minor or World copy targets with structured errors, and keep child controls aligned with existing power-specific staging.
close_reason: done

title: Audit major power choices and unavailable browser states
description: Audit move-power-options, prompts, ready-state checks, and move-transition-unavailable behavior for all major arcana whose pure facades are implemented. Ensure cards with partially staged browser support show clear power choices, intentionally unwired powers preserve explicit unavailable results instead of silent no-ops, and README, AGENTS, and MIND_MAP stay accurate as browser support changes.
close_reason: Audited major power choices, fixed World copied Disc/Sword target filtering, and added regression coverage for unavailable fallback and copied Death staging.

title: Initial piece drag and drop does not place piece
description:
Actual: When the current player has no pieces and drags the first/initial small piece onto a territory or wasteland, dropping it does not place the piece.

Expected: Dropping the initial placement piece onto a legal empty territory or legal empty wasteland should stage or confirm the same initial-placement command path as Detailed entry/CSS fallback drop, resulting in the current player small piece being placed and the pending/turn-action state updating according to the existing direct manipulation contract.

Repro notes: Start a normal local game from the lobby so the current player has no pieces on the board. Drag the first/initial placement piece to an empty territory or wasteland and drop. The piece is not placed on either target type.

Scope: Direct manipulation initial placement drop handling for both territory and wasteland targets. Check Three.js and CSS fallback paths, gesture-intent staging, pending tray confirmation behavior, and that apply-initial-placement remains the only gameplay mutation boundary.

Related: bd-2vcc tracks missing destination highlighting during the same initial-piece drag flow.

Related docs: README.md Gameplay Move Selection, AGENTS.md move-selection guidance, MIND_MAP.md nodes [14], [24], [37], [43], and [51].
close_reason: Verified current implementation and added regression coverage for territory and wasteland initial-placement direct drops.

comments.author: David Plumpton
comments.text: Verified current behavior: stash-piece initial-placement gestures stage without mutating game state, confirm through apply-initial-placement, and place the current player's small piece on both wasteland and territory targets. Added app-state regression coverage for the previously missing territory-target assertion. Also reran clojure -M:test and clojure -M:smoke; the existing smoke covers confirmed Three.js and CSS direct drops.

title: Correct README claims about unsupported multiplayer and state import/export
description: README.md:3 says users can play through a common server or export and send/import game state. The current Ring server only serves static resources, and there is no multiplayer sync or import/export workflow in src or test. Update the README to describe current capabilities or add implementation docs once those features exist. Also fix the send/sent typo while touching that sentence.
close_reason: done

title: Render Icehouse pyramid pieces on the 3D board
description: Implement visible Icehouse pyramid/minion pieces in the Three.js board scene. Context: README.md and MIND_MAP.md describe the current 3D board as textured tarot card planes only; docs/rules.txt defines Icehouse pieces as player-colored minions with size/pips and five legal orientations. Acceptance criteria: pieces render on top of occupied territory and wasteland spaces without hiding card art; geometry communicates size/pips, player color, and upright/cardinal orientation; placement supports up to three pieces in one space; rendering is driven by shared game state rather than duplicated scene-only data; OrbitControls and card raycast selection continue to work; CSS fallback remains usable or clearly omits unsupported 3D piece rendering; advanced compilation externs are updated for any new Three.js APIs; verification includes clojure -M:test, clojure -M:release, and browser smoke checks for desktop/mobile nonblank canvas, visible pieces, orientation changes, spacing, orbit/zoom/reset, and selection panel behavior.
notes: Implementation: added shared gnostica.pieces state for player colors, sizes/pips, orientations, and grouped board-space occupants; rendered pieces as lit Three.js cone meshes over card planes; added CSS fallback piece markers and selected-territory piece details; added WebGL renderer failure fallback. Verification: clojure -M:test passed with 9 tests and 51 assertions; clojure -M:release completed with 0 shadow-cljs warnings; CDP smoke against released app on headless Chrome with SwiftShader at 1280x900 and 390x800 loaded Three r128/OrbitControls, rendered non-fallback canvases, visible cards and colored pyramid pieces, reset button, and selected-territory piece list. Also verified WebGL-context failure falls back to the CSS board with visible piece markers on desktop/mobile.
close_reason: Implemented shared Icehouse piece state, Three.js pyramid mesh rendering, CSS fallback markers, selected-territory piece details, WebGL renderer fallback, tests, release build, and desktop/mobile smoke verification.

title: Document CHROME_PATH alongside SMOKE_CHROME for smoke checks
description: The smoke runner accepts both SMOKE_CHROME and CHROME_PATH when locating Chrome or Chromium, and its missing-browser error reports both checked env vars. README.md and AGENTS.md only mention SMOKE_CHROME. Either document CHROME_PATH too or deliberately remove support so the docs and runtime stay aligned.
close_reason: done

title: Centralize move power and control metadata
description: Move power knowledge is scattered across source/power definitions in src/main/gnostica/move_selection.cljc around lines 41-70, option selection around 1977-2049, control group selection around 2223-2289, command building around 6274-6305, transition dispatch around 6821-6888, UI rendering in src/main/gnostica/ui/move_panel.cljs around 1014-1203, and app-state view aggregation around src/main/gnostica/app_state.cljc lines 1360-1491. Adding or changing a power requires editing many case statements and keeping a source-parsing test in sync. Acceptance: introduce a data-driven registry for each implemented power that captures label, available controls, command builder, transition facade, preview capability, and renderer/control key where practical. Remove duplicated case branches or cover them from the registry.
close_reason: Centralized move power metadata in gnostica.move-selection.registry and routed controls, command builders, transitions, previewability, renderer coverage, and docs through it.

title: Remove remaining vector-position board lookups from browser subscriptions and labels
description:
The stable board-index rule is not fully applied in browser wiring. src/main/gnostica/app/events.cljs still derives selected-board-cell with (get cells selected-index), while app-state uses layout/cell-by-index. src/main/gnostica/ui/move_panel.cljs piece-choice-label also uses (get board (:space-index piece)). After territory destruction leaves gaps, or Cup appends board index 9, the territory panel and move piece labels can show the wrong card or unknown space even though app-state and move-selection use stable indexes elsewhere.

Acceptance criteria:
- Browser subscriptions and move-panel labels use a shared board-index lookup helper or receive already-resolved labels from the app-state view model.
- Add a re-frame subscription or view-model test for a destroyed-board gap and an appended board index.
- rg over src/main no longer finds board-cell lookup patterns that treat stable board indexes as vector positions.
close_reason: Fixed selected-board-cell and move-panel piece labels to resolve stable board indexes, with gap/appended territory view-model coverage.

title: Eliminate dispatch-sync from renderer lifecycle callbacks
description:
Fresh-eye re-frame layer review: app.cljs passes :on-clear-texture-errors as #(rf/dispatch-sync [::clear-three-texture-errors]) at line 475, and three_board.cljs calls that callback from mount! at line 886. dispatch-sync is generally only appropriate for initialization or tests; using it from a renderer lifecycle callback bypasses the normal event queue and makes render/mount ordering part of app-db semantics.

Acceptance criteria:
- Remove dispatch-sync from Three.js lifecycle callbacks.
- Use normal dispatch, an explicit event/fx flow, or app-db state derived from renderer identity so stale texture errors clear without synchronous mutation.
- Add or update tests/smoke checks to cover remount and icon-mode toggling without stale texture errors.
close_reason: Implemented queued texture-error clearing and smoke coverage

title: Add browser Fool reveal-play controls
description:
README.md currently notes that browser Fool staging reveals cards without nested revealed-card play commands, while the pure rule engine already supports `apply-fool-move` reveal actions containing `:command` or `:play-command` maps routed through implemented suit and full-card major powers. `docs/rules.txt` says Fool turns over the next draw-pile card and may play it, then may repeat once; the browser should let the player make that choice after seeing each revealed card.

Implement the most natural browser flow for Fool reveal-play while keeping it aligned with the existing staged move-selection architecture. A good target shape is a sequential Fool wizard in `gnostica.move-selection` and `gnostica.app-state`: choose the Fool source as today, reveal up to two cards, show each revealed card, let the player choose Skip or Play, reuse the existing detailed controls/action ribbon to build the revealed card move, then proceed to the next reveal. Model revealed cards as already-discarded temporary sources so the final command can call `apply-fool-move` with nested reveal commands and so revealed cards are not charged twice.

Important design constraints:
- Do not expose draw-pile card identity before the player has committed to revealing that card.
- Prefer preserving the existing invariant that `:game` is not mutated before final Confirm. If a committed mid-turn reveal is necessary, represent it explicitly as pending Fool turn state with documented rollback/cancel behavior and tests.
- Reuse existing source, power, minion, target, replacement, orientation, and action-ribbon controls where possible instead of building Fool-specific duplicates.
- Keep World-copy-of-Fool behavior on the same path when World copies a Fool territory.
- Keep transition randomness at the existing app event boundary by passing the injected `:shuffle-fn` when Fool reveal needs discard reshuffle.

Acceptance criteria:
- Browser move selection can complete a Fool move that skips both reveals, plays the first reveal and skips the second, skips the first and plays the second, and plays two reveals where legal.
- Revealed minor suit cards can be played through the existing Cup/Rod/Disc/Sword controls, including target/replacement/orientation validation and structured rejection display.
- Revealed implemented major cards can be played through the same full-card major controls available for normal hand or territory sources, including ordered major ribbons where applicable.
- Final confirmation builds an `apply-fool-move` command with `:reveals` containing the correct skipped maps and nested play commands, charges the original Fool source exactly once, treats revealed cards as already discarded, preserves unique card accounting, records the turn action once, and leaves rejected confirmations staged without mutating `:game`.
- The UI clearly shows the current Fool reveal step, revealed card title/art, Skip/Play choices, completed skipped/played steps, and remaining reveal count in the action ribbon or pending tray.
- App-state or move-selection tests cover command construction and game-state results for skipped, mixed skip/play, and two-play flows, plus World-copy Fool if practical. Existing `clojure -M:test` must pass.
close_reason: Implemented browser Fool reveal-play controls with staged skip/play commands and tests
